Recommended computer science courses and recommended learning order
Single variable calculus: MIT Calculus I
Multivariable calculus: MIT Calculus II
Probability: MITx Probability - The Science of Uncertainty and Data
Differential equations: MIT Differential Equations
Signals and systems: MIT Signals

rential Equations
Signals and systems: MIT Signals and systems
Linear algebra: MIT Linear algebra
Intro to discrete mathematics: MIT Mathematics for computer science
Classical mechanics: RiceX mechanics part 1, part 2
Electricity and magnetism: RiceX Electricity and magnetism part 1, part 2
Computer

Electricity and magnetism part 1, part 2
Computer literacy: MIT CS missing semester
Introduction to computer science: Teachyourselfcs programming recommendations
Computer architecture: Teachyourselfcs computer architecture recommendations
Operating systems: Teachyourselfcs operating systems recomme

systems: Teachyourselfcs operating systems recommendations
Computer networks: Teachyourselfcs networks recommendations
Data structures and algorithms: Teachyourselfcs algorithms recommendations
Languages and compilers: Teachyourselfcs languages recommendations
Software construction: MIT Software con

mendations
Software construction: MIT Software construction
Software engineering: UBCx Software engineering
Machine learning: Andrew Ng Machine Learning
Image processing and computer vision: NU Fundamentals of Digital Image and Video Processing
Computer graphics: UCSDx Computer graphics
Databases: T

ter graphics: UCSDx Computer graphics
Databases: Teachyourselfcs databases recommendations
HTML, CSS and JS: John Hopkins HTML, CSS, and Javascript for Web Developers
CSS frameworks: Front-End Web UI Frameworks and Tools: Bootstrap 4
Back-end frameworks: Server-side Development with NodeJS, Express

rks: Server-side Development with NodeJS, Express and MongoDB
Front-end frameworks: Front-End JavaScript Frameworks: Angular
Introduction to computer security: MIT Computer systems security
How many of these courses have you completed? You might be interested in the Open-Source Computer Science degr

nterested in the Open-Source Computer Science degree, which is a similar concept to what you've made here. You can contribute to their Github if you think that they should add something to their curriculum.
For those wondering, this is an extremely good tool for those wanting to be solid in their un

ood tool for those wanting to be solid in their undergrad knowledge before moving to masters, or for those wishing to switch from another major to understand what they need to know before going into a masters program. If you want a basic undergrad level job though, getting that piece of paper is a l

vel job though, getting that piece of paper is a lot more important as you first need to get through HR and they look for keywords since they don't know what they are really screening for.
You're right that you don't come away with the piece of paper at the end of the degree, which is a huge part of

at the end of the degree, which is a huge part of what you're paying for in a university CS education. The courses are designed to give you the knowledge that you could get by completing a CS degree. It's structured in a progression similar to a bachelors in Computer Science, but in a free online f

helors in Computer Science, but in a free online format.
I recommend it because for my CS education, I'm doing a combination of the two approaches. I've completed several of the courses in the open-source curriculum, but I decided that I still want to get a traditional university degree to be more e

o get a traditional university degree to be more employable as well.
I highly recommend doing this for any CS student who can commit enough time to juggle both. Supplementing your university courses with these online ones will give you knowledge that you might not otherwise get and an advantage in y

you might not otherwise get and an advantage in your normal university courses. CS50 in particular is the best of all the courses that I've done so far, including the in-person ones that I'm paying for.
I agree. CS50 Is worth the time investment of all the online programming courses I've tried.
I l

all the online programming courses I've tried.
I like CS50 a little more than TOP mostly because of David's lectures and the teaching style. TOP really helps shore up some basic dev skills like being fairly proficient with the command line, git/github workflow and getting you set up with a VM runnin

b workflow and getting you set up with a VM running linux as well as real world IDEs. It cant hurt to do both but I would focus on CS50 first.
The main benefit of a degree is landing interviews. It's possible to break into the industry without a degree, but it's significantly harder. It used to be t

ee, but it's significantly harder. It used to be that university education was really the only way to learn computer science, but there are so many resources online today that it's much less necessary.
A lot of it depends on where you are in the world as well. In my area, the vast majority of the jo

d as well. In my area, the vast majority of the job listings will include a bachelors degree in their requirements, whether that's a CS degree or some other degree matters a lot less.
Is there really a need for that much emphasis on mathematics? I've done inferential statistics, different number sys

done inferential statistics, different number systems and calculus over 3 years so far in college but this seems like a lot.
Yeah, most CS majors don't need this much math. I should make a course filter depend on goals.
For anything 3D, you'll need to understand math pretty much perfectly. Also for

to understand math pretty much perfectly. Also for most of the solutions you build, using a geometric model will be pretty much required.
It really depends on what you want to do after your degree. There's a ton of software that doesn't require heavy duty math. But then again, most students don't kn

duty math. But then again, most students don't know exactly what kind of software they want to get into or what that actually requires.
You do not need anywhere near that amount of math. Take Single Variable Calculus, take Math for Computer Science, take the rest only as needed (with the possible e

take the rest only as needed (with the possible exception of Linear Algebra, which is probably worth taking regardless).
I'm not sure about this, but I don't think you should take Missing Semester of CS before doing any courses. That should probably go after at least your first course so you have a

go after at least your first course so you have a bit of background.
You're using the TeachYourselfCS sequence, but that was created for people who have already been working and have experience programming, just want to learn the cs stuff to get better. If that's not you, add 61B in between the int

ter. If that's not you, add 61B in between the intro cs and computer architecture sections.
I'm almost done with my CS degree and this post is making me feel mad insecure about my math knowledge.
Nah, don't stress it. This is a very ideal breadth and depth of coverage, that's good for self-study to

depth of coverage, that's good for self-study to fill in whatever gaps there are in your foundation once you're in industry or applying for PhD programs.
I would probably remove the physics and the signals and systems courses. They are not relevant to CS unless you want to do some kind of 3D modeli

to CS unless you want to do some kind of 3D modeling or apply programming to a different engineering field. Strictly CS, totally unnecessary.
From a mathematics perspective I wouldn't bother with the calculus, probability, or diff eq for the same reasons above. You are missing a proofs, abstract alg

sons above. You are missing a proofs, abstract algebra, and number theory course. Also, make sure your data and algorithms course covers P vs NP. Most do, but I didn't look through the syllabus for these courses.
Take out multi variable calc, diff EQ, signals, and then elec and mag. Those are more C

, signals, and then elec and mag. Those are more Comp engineering courses.
I'm due to graduate soon, and I can tell you this covers about 60% of my degree. The usual boot camp stuff covers less than 5%. If you don't half ass it and do everything on here, you'll definitely know as much as an undergra

ere, you'll definitely know as much as an undergraduate, maybe even more. However, without a degree to prove it, I'm wondering if it's really worth doing all of this at home.
I agree with everything in your course graph except for the paths leading from the Software Engineering, HTML CSS JS, and Dat

rom the Software Engineering, HTML CSS JS, and Databases nodes in the tree. These aren't really computer science. So I wouldn't include these unless someone desires to go into software development and/or web development as they aren't really necessary to learn unless you are going into that industry
y to learn unless you are going into that industry.

Best course to have good foundation in computer science
I definitely recommend Harvard's CS50 since you can just do it for free online.
Hello! I taught programming for a while and still doing it to this day. What is taught and how it is taught changes through the years.
Some sort of course on basics

s through the years.
Some sort of course on basics of programming, preferably in C. Some exposure to CPU architecture.
Basics of Java/C#/C++ and OOP. Two courses.
Data structures and algorithms. Two courses.
Course on software development and design.
Extremely important today - multicore programming

Extremely important today - multicore programming.
This is very basic skills you need to work in today's SWE.
With that said, someone in my circle of friends became super successful product manager in mid size software company with history degree, coming on board knowing nothing about SWE. If your

oming on board knowing nothing about SWE. If your goal is to become manager at software engineering company, take few basic courses in any programming languages, basic course on software design, course on cloud and databases.
Just a career advice: startups failure rate is astonishing. Be prepared to

artups failure rate is astonishing. Be prepared to jump around.
If it's python then I highly recommend going through this book called "Automate the Boring Stuff with Python". Python is a good start but python programmers are the first ones to be replaced by AI as it can generate highly accurate code

aced by AI as it can generate highly accurate code. You can probably pick some other niche technologies.
Don't agree. Programmers won't be replaced by AI. The real takeaway is that knowing a language isn't enough, you need fundamentals. Writing maintainable code, designing scalable systems, understa

ainable code, designing scalable systems, understanding tradeoffs, etc. Learning a language is fun and definitely useful but it's not enough to start an engineering career. Also it's important to specify that there's a big difference between simply making python scripts and actually developing a sys

aking python scripts and actually developing a system using python.
A script runs, does a task once, and then you automate it or move on. A system has to keep running, deal with errors, maybe talk to a database or API, support more than one user, and generally stick around. Once you're thinking abou

generally stick around. Once you're thinking about tests, logging, scaling, or security, you're designing a system.
I disagree as well. AI won't fully replace software developers. Learn the language that's right for the goal you're trying to achieve. It's actually a good thing if AI is good at your

. It's actually a good thing if AI is good at your language, it can help you learn it faster.
It can help you learn faster but it can also hinder growth. If you use it to understand code better and to do tedious tasks more quickly it's great. But I've also seen people just throw every issue that com

e also seen people just throw every issue that comes up into a prompt until the problem is solved, you don't learn much by doing that.
Maybe you could do a masters in computer science? That way you could leverage that into an internship, which many startups prefer you to have plus professional exper

tartups prefer you to have plus professional experience.
Yeah, these are kind of pseudo-bachelor's degree. All they care about is any college degree, and you get out in fewer years. The master's degree in the US is strange. There are professional masters which are mostly money-making. It's a 1-2 yea

ters which are mostly money-making. It's a 1-2 year program, and is just more courses. There's the classic masters which leads to a PhD. Georgia Tech has such a master's in computer science.
There are a bunch of schools that have CS Masters for people without a BA in CS in the US.
Computer Science i

e without a BA in CS in the US.
Computer Science is no more about computers than astronomy is about telescopes. - Edgar Dijkstra. Computer Science is not equal to Programming. Programming is a part of Computer Science though.
Computer Science is the study of computation, information, and automation.

study of computation, information, and automation. Notice that all three do not require computers. We've been computing, studying information, and automating certain tasks before written history.
Take courses from big universities. CS61A and CS61B will give a really good foundation.
I've been lookin

ll give a really good foundation.
I've been looking up this question for a while and I came to find this is the first course students would take at MIT: Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs. The whole idea is the logic behind programming, it doesn't matter the language.
You can use the

, it doesn't matter the language.
You can use the GT OMSCS minimum requirements as a guide for what foundational CS knowledge you need.

Advice for Computer Science student
Hey guys I was wondering what would be the best advice for students in Computer Science. I have taken only my first two programming courses Intro to C++, and Object Orientated and struggled since I have never programmed before but I have taken other courses like P

ammed before but I have taken other courses like Programming Language Concepts, Discrete Structures which I really liked and enjoyed and understood the material. I guess I'm asking what's the best advice to become a better programmer? FYI my next programming course will be Data Structures. Thanks!
gramming course will be Data Structures. Thanks!

The design of a wholesome introduction to computer science course
The internet is flooded with questions about which introductory book or course is the best to start with, which language to choose, etc. There are too many resources out there and each of them has its own shortcomings.
One common tren

of them has its own shortcomings.
One common trend I have seen is that people want to start with a very high-level language and then straight move on to an Algorithms and Datastructures resource. One common complaint that is also in abundance in the forums is that after this process they still don'

forums is that after this process they still don't understand how computers work under the hood; the most common complaint being "I don't quite get how pointers work".
My point is that I don't get one thing. Why don't universities design an introductory course that makes use of languages starting f

tory course that makes use of languages starting from high-level languages to assembly (even if TOY languages and TOY hardware). The course focuses on a few basic DS and Algos. The students, then, can learn about programming, comp architecture and computer science in a wholesome package at all level

mputer science in a wholesome package at all levels. When you see the whole picture at once, the maps formed in your mind get really strong and well connected.
Two books that mirror this ideology are SICP and Nand2Tetris. Colleges have stopped using SICP as an intro text long back. No US college fol

SICP as an intro text long back. No US college follows this principle. In fact, no new books are written with that in mind nowadays. Every book is about learning X language and solving a set of problems in that language.
Why has CS education been watered down over the years? Only to create giant wor

ered down over the years? Only to create giant workforces for the industry? Where did the joy of CS vanish?
Computers and languages were built from the ground up starting from transistors and digital logic. Adding layer upon layer of abstractions we got to where we are currently. Why don't universit

got to where we are currently. Why don't universities follow that process in the introductory course rather than teaching some recently popular high level language to build things? Why not teach CS starting that way with appropriate history thrown in at each stage to justify design decisions. Then p

at each stage to justify design decisions. Then programming wouldn't be a headache in later courses and the later courses may focus on more math and theory part of CS.
I studied electrical engineering and computer science. I've found that I have a much better understanding of how computers actually

uch better understanding of how computers actually work even though I'm not as knowledgeable in data structures etc. I took courses in semiconductor physics, circuits, signal processing, assembly language, and the basic cs courses. It's really cool that I have enough knowledge to explain how a basic

hat I have enough knowledge to explain how a basic processor works from the tiniest particles to UI interfaces. Highly recommend any CS students out there take a few electrical engineering courses.
I studied mechatronics and had the same experience. On my course we first studied digital logic, basic

On my course we first studied digital logic, basic computer architecture and then assembly before moving on to C and finally C++.
Back in my day the CS courses started out with Modula 2 and you were expected to take an assembly course in the third or fourth semester. It is a good approach because be

fourth semester. It is a good approach because before you get too deep into data structures and other advanced concepts you get exposure to a simple controller coding and what is actually going on with the processor.
I did Harvard CS50 Intro to Comp Sci. We started off with C and clawed our way up

Sci. We started off with C and clawed our way up to Python, all the while learning about elementary data structures and algorithms. Towards the latter part we learned about some basic web development. They had a really abstracted explanation on what was going on under the hood, more specifically wi

was going on under the hood, more specifically with RAM. That actually helped quite a bit when dealing with pointers and memory allocation. Didn't really cover anything on computer architecture though which is a bummer but to be expected. I thought it was a decent intro to CS.
I think CS50 does it

it was a decent intro to CS.
I think CS50 does it in the best way, as an intro course that anyone can take, starting with a low level language and abstracting from there.
Yes CS50 covers much of the whole picture. But it is quite shallow.
At my University they had a course called programming which w

rsity they had a course called programming which was about abstraction. We weren't even writing working code at first. Weren't even using a computer. This was very much about not knowing what's under the hood. That course later on evolved into learning about algorithmic complexity and data structure

ng about algorithmic complexity and data structures, recursion and functional programming. It used a programming language designed specifically for learning.
On the other hand, you had a course called machines. This started with teaching about logic operators and then proceeded to hardware: diodes,

operators and then proceeded to hardware: diodes, flipflops, then NAND ICs and plugging that stuff on a powered board to make a program this way. This course later evolved into basic CPU workings, writing code in 1s and 0s with opcodes and registers. Then we made an assembler and moved to writing as

Then we made an assembler and moved to writing assembly. Then writing and compiling a lib to be linked with a C-like language and ultimately have runnable code on an actual computer. Later on, that class moved to basic operating system architecture and modding a Unix variant called minix.
The first

and modding a Unix variant called minix.
The first semester of my first undergrad year had a Computer Architecture course covering logic-gates, micro architectures, ISAs and higher-level software abstractions. We did not really see the electrical-engineering part but designed a lot of cool stuff, st

ineering part but designed a lot of cool stuff, starting from basic logic gates up to an assembler, written in C. The course was partially based on nand2tetris.
CS is no more about inventing. It is all about salaries, Silicon valley and career fairs.
I guess one of the biggest reasons is that new st

I guess one of the biggest reasons is that new students are starting out by learning languages like python or javascript because they're easy. We should be learning languages like C, because even though it is hard, it manages to teach all topics clearly, it's statically typed, pointer-use heavy and

arly, it's statically typed, pointer-use heavy and low level and these things make it better for learning as a first language. You will face no problem learning a new language once you know C. Also there should be a computer history class where you can learn what older techniques were and why they w

an learn what older techniques were and why they were wrong and how we solved them.
Any subject should have a history class. How it came to be? How it evolved and why it evolved? Stories remain etched easily in your memory.
I think it varies from one place to another. At my university we started wit

place to another. At my university we started with C and that was my introduction to programming and computer science. It was only much later that I took an OOP course which introduced the concept of OOP using Java. I even learned some basic assembly language before ever touching Java. I think it w

y language before ever touching Java. I think it would have been way more difficult to learn C after Java than it was the other way around.
layer upon layer of abstractions is exactly what CS is about and it is important to appreciate each layer. CS is not about programming and there is no need to l

is not about programming and there is no need to learn any industrial programming language. Each of those is just a middle layer of many layers that start from digital logic. Whatever layer you look at it seems to be the computer, and the layer below it is the computer after a level of abstraction a

it is the computer after a level of abstraction and so on to the lowest level of logic gates.
My university has a course, advanced programming, designed to do this. The course design was submitted and published in some pedagogy journals.
How do you feel about Crash Course Computer Science on YouTub
feel about Crash Course Computer Science on YouTube? It is usually my go-to when someone asks me where to start with CS.

This is a very difficult class but if you intend to take the foundation exam take it with Tanvir, his class is well organized by quizzes, assignments, labs and reviews so you'll learn a looot, there is no way you won't understand. But be prepared eitherways with any professor it is hard. Whole class

herways with any professor it is hard. Whole class is based on data structure al algorithms so linked list, DMA, binary,etc...
Comments on the professor
He really wants to make sure you learn
Linked lists and Hash Tables, and binary search are like your bread and butter of intermediate leetcode prob

our bread and butter of intermediate leetcode problems so this class is basically 70% of what you'll need for your generic technical interview
Comments on the professor
You stanford kids are lucky you got Szumlanski. He's the goat.
Be ready for it to kick you in the behind unless you're studying and

kick you in the behind unless you're studying and working out your assignments almost daily. First big boy CS class at UCF
Going into CS1 at the start of the semester, I knew this was going to be a challenging course. Not just the material and the methods of the exam, but also Szumlanski himself is

ethods of the exam, but also Szumlanski himself is known for being a difficult professor. I can safely say that this is known information for a reason: it's true. This class was brutal sometimes, with many of my programming assignments being massive headaches to get through (if I got through them at

headaches to get through (if I got through them at all). However, Szumlanski does make the material understandable and digestible if you put in the effort and go to class. This is not a class you can just breeze through, but if you buckle down and study, you'll be just fine.
Lots of data structures

udy, you'll be just fine.
Lots of data structures and runtime analysis in this class. There's some components that get skipped over or watered down until you take CS2, but for the most part it's all relevant enough that you'll be using the information taught for quite a while in your upper level cla

n taught for quite a while in your upper level classes.
Comments on the professor
Szumlanski is an absolute treat of a professor, and a true gem within the CS department. He knows the ins and outs of the material covered in class, and the way he explains stuff makes it make so much sense compared to

ains stuff makes it make so much sense compared to how I learned it in the past.
The main con with Szumlanski is that grading takes a while for your assignments, but he has explained that part of that is due to lack of available funding for TA's, which is understandable.
Office hours, while very use

ch is understandable.
Office hours, while very useful, are often limited to certain days of the week. As such, this can limit your ability to get help for a programming assignment if you procrastinate on it. So don't procrastinate.
Want to figure out if CS is for you? Worried about the Foundation Ex

if CS is for you? Worried about the Foundation Exam? Just interested in coding? Then this class is for you! Szum’s lectures are awesome and he will prepare you well for the FE (he even used to work for Google!). Be warned though: the learning curve is very steep and things go at a fast pace. You wi

is very steep and things go at a fast pace. You will have to keep yourself in check in order to not fall behind (that means practicing your code/coding parts of your projects nearly every day). Projects are given out every two weeks, so make sure you start them when he hands them out!
This class is

start them when he hands them out!
This class is also very math and logic-heavy and that’s the main thing that weeds people out of CS (sadly, myself included). Make sure you brush up on pointers and linked lists as well!
Szumlanski's class is very difficult, but very vital if you're looking to gain

ifficult, but very vital if you're looking to gain the knowledge you'd actually want for a job in the field. The course has very fair grading, but don't expect to put in no work and get a C. Highly recommend.
Read each assignment's PDF the night it comes out and work on it a little every day. Eustis

omes out and work on it a little every day. Eustis was down at pivotal moments, so you'd want to be sure to get the assignments done as fast as possible.
As everyone knows, Szum is an amazing professor. If you have the chance to take ANY class with him, do it!
The class involves algorithms and data

im, do it!
The class involves algorithms and data structures using the C programming language. Algorithms include sorting and backtracking and some stuff with bitwise operators in C. Data structures are Linked Lists, stacks, queues, binary trees, avl trees, hash tables, and maybe a couple others I’m

trees, hash tables, and maybe a couple others I’m forgetting.
Szum will also prepare you really well for the foundation exam if you’re a CS student.
Assignments take a long time but are definitely doable. There are a ton of available TAs for help, but personally I prefer Szum‘s office hours.
Start

ut personally I prefer Szum‘s office hours.
Start on assignments early, prepare for tests (even if you normally don’t, cuz I normally don’t), don’t he afraid to go to office hours and ask questions.
You might be wondering why I put 1/5 for “easy” but “5/5” for interesting. That’s because Szum is a f

“5/5” for interesting. That’s because Szum is a fantastic professor, but the material itself is absolute murder.
The class hinges on the C programming language and math at the level of at least Calculus with Analytical Geometry.
With my multiple years of programming experience, I was able to easily

rs of programming experience, I was able to easily do the C programming part but I bombed the math parts of the two exams I took because I had terrible math professors in the past and failed those classes entirely.
I had a 70% with a project and an exam still ungraded by the time the withdrawal dead

xam still ungraded by the time the withdrawal deadline came around, so I didn’t take my chances and I withdrew from the course. If Szum had graded those two things a little bit faster, I might have stayed in if I was more certain about my grad
Learn math before taking this class. That’s the part tha

math before taking this class. That’s the part that gets you.
This course is taught using C which is used in many other upper year courses in CS like computer organization and operating systems

Never Respond to emails. Takes forever to post grades (almost 2 months and we don't have grades for HW1). Does not prepare for lectures most of the time, and you can tell because he gets stuck on a slide trying to figure it out. Some student questions go unanswered because he adds texts to the slide

s go unanswered because he adds texts to the slides that he does not know. Slow communicator. Often talks about other things not related to the topic. on hand. He does not explain much some topics. Many mistakes on the exams. Promises to do things and never does. Lack of integrity. He is not a teach

d never does. Lack of integrity. He is not a teacher at all. Stay away. You will not learn much. Student could not decide whether to withdraw the class because they did not know their grades. He struggles with Zoom. I can't believe UCF still hires him. Read the Rate my professor website.
If you HAVE

m. Read the Rate my professor website.
If you HAVE to take him, go to office hours, use the slides to know the topics and research and learn about them on your own. Record the lecture and watch it in 1.7x the speed (he speaks slowly). He gives some review problems but that is not enough to be prepar

eview problems but that is not enough to be prepared for the exams
Meade continues to struggle effectively teaching at UCF. He is without a doubt very knowledgeable about Computer Science, and he would be a great person to talk about all types of theory and general topics in conversation. But, when

ory and general topics in conversation. But, when we are trying to do assignments and pass exams, Meade is by far one of the worst at preparing us to do well. The cutoff for passing was a 50%, with 4 dropped assignments out of 10 total (3 labs, 7 Programming Assignments) and 3 tests. Unless you are

gramming Assignments) and 3 tests. Unless you are truly passionate, you will feel lost with his method of teaching and be hopelessly bored watching him try to explain things.
Don't fall behind, because you will not pass this class if you do. There is way too much course material for any sort of slac

way too much course material for any sort of slacking. Stay around the curve and focus on the labs and easy assignments, these can set you up early to do well. And don't cheat, he gives negative grades instead of 0's.
Gerber is a nice professor. This is the first time he taught this class and it di

s is the first time he taught this class and it did show a little bit. Lectures were online through zoom and were recorded for later viewing. Delivery was mostly reading powerpoints with not very much live coding. Only reason I put 4/5 stars for easy were because of the programming assignments. They

were because of the programming assignments. They can be challenging but definitely doable.
Start early on assignments. He makes homework and programming assignments very difficult to teach you. Exams are rather easy if you know the content.

Introduction To Computer Science I
If you take this class, make sure to read the reviews about who your professor is going to be. This class is so important for your foundation to learning harder computer classes. Besides that, the assignments are quite easy and if you have supplemental resources li

ite easy and if you have supplemental resources like Chegg, StackOverflow, etc… you can definitely succeed!
Comments on the professor
Evelyn Lulis has MANY negative reviews on RateMyProfessors as well. And for good reason, too. She rambles nonsense a lot, assumes the worst of her student, and is ove

lot, assumes the worst of her student, and is overall just really negative.
I would have taken the class with a better professor.
I went into this class with some Python experience, which I really think helped me do as well as I did in this class. Lots of little projects for weekly assignments, I t

ots of little projects for weekly assignments, I took this class once a week in a 3 hour block, which really made lectures feel like they went on forever.
Basic Python information, I highly recommend taking notes and reviewing vocabulary if it's your first time looking at/writing code.
Comments on t

first time looking at/writing code.
Comments on the professor
Saweh's lectures put me to sleep. Again, I did take this class in a 3 hour block, from 6-9pm which I definitely would not recommend. The class itself wasn't terrible, but I did not get a lot out of Saweh's lectures.
Comments on the profe

lot out of Saweh's lectures.
Comments on the professor

Very organized and efficient! Tests and quizzes are fair, no tricks. Clear lectures, many online resources, many tutors available. A lot is learned through online videos that are then expanded on in the lectures, so if you don't like that maybe avoid. Easy to get a good grade if you just do the work

. Easy to get a good grade if you just do the work.
He is the best professor that I am going to ever have in nyu, I knew this since the first week I have him. I take this course as an elective and choose cs and learn python because of him. Really love Prof. Kapp, take him if u can!!!!!!!!!!!!
only 1

e Prof. Kapp, take him if u can!!!!!!!!!!!!
only 1 month in and i already know he's the best professor ill ever have in nyu
Professor Kapp was incredible! He gave me such a good intro to the computer programming world.

Had him for 304 and 481. I found both courses pretty difficult overall. For 304, I felt Prof. Decker did a good job giving us a lot of practice to help the concepts click.
